title: What if Asuka was a clone in NGE
display_title: What if Asuka was a clone in NGE
wikitext:
[]: What if Asuka was a clone in NGE?             Quote Post by C.T.1290 » Thu Sep 02, 2021 10:16 pm Okay, now I know that this part of the storyline should remain exclusive to rebuild, but a certain thought has occurred to me. What if Soryu is actually the same as Rei? Would that change things rather drastically if they had use this idea in NGE? I know many people would prefer Asuka as a human being rather than a clone. I don’t think it would take away her characteristics, she would still be Asuka after all.

[]: Re: What if Asuka was a clone in NGE?             Quote Post by Jinroh » Fri Sep 03, 2021 2:37 am It would remove the problematic relationship with her mother that defines her. Also in the manga she was a test tube baby, it may have been the same in the show, just not explicitly stated. In a way she may have always been engineered to be a pilot.

[]: Re: What if Asuka was a clone in NGE?             Quote Post by Bhorium » Fri Sep 03, 2021 8:36 am Jinroh wrote: it may have been the same in the show

[]: Re: What if Asuka was a clone in NGE?             Quote Post by RussianRiz » Fri Sep 03, 2021 9:44 am It would change some central interpretive aspects that we have as a base today. I think with that we could even go so far as to imply that Shinji is a clone too. Maybe we would get a new cyberpunk narrative? Just like Ghost in the Shell? It reminds me that a few years ago, there was a more or less popular theory that children born after Second Impact had no souls. It seems to me that this type of proposal ends up going too far from the scope that the original series wants to employ and gets too attached to mythological aspects of the franchise, which are more an aesthetic and derivative thing than real significant to the plot.

[]: Re: What if Asuka was a clone in NGE?             Quote Post by Archer » Fri Sep 03, 2021 10:22 am Bhorium wrote: Simply put, Asuka was not made special, she chose to become special in response to the cards life had handed her. She chose to become dependent on the Evangelion in an admittedly somewhat misguided attempt to rebuild her self-esteem after all the tragedy and trauma that had befallen her early in life. There is a pretty powerful and interesting story there, and the idea of her being being a specially engineered clone, bred for battle, would go against that.

[]: Re: What if Asuka was a clone in NGE?             Quote Post by Bhorium » Fri Sep 03, 2021 10:40 am Oh, I completely agree with your reading of Gendo's motivations here. I meant to imply that Yui was the one who had greater plans for Shinji from the beginning. I would also add that Yui also set herself to play a special role by arranging to getting absorbed by the only Lilith-based Eva. Eva-02 is, at least in theory, really just another production model based on Adam, and it might just be how I read things, but I got the sense that it was drastically easier/possible to replace the core in the production models.

[]: Re: What if Asuka was a clone in NGE?             Quote Post by Raikyu » Fri Sep 03, 2021 10:46 am Jinroh wrote: Also in the manga she was a test tube baby, it may have been the same in the show, just not explicitly stated. In a way she may have always been engineered to be a pilot.

[]: Re: What if Asuka was a clone in NGE?             Quote Post by Archer » Fri Sep 03, 2021 10:52 am I was actually going to start a thread regarding the “service life” of Evas, specifically with regards to the replaceability of their cores. Because if they CAN’T be replaced, then each Eva (which, back-of-the-napkin estimates based on real-life vehicles of a similar size, costs somewhere in the neighborhood of the several hundreds of millions of dollars) would have an effective service life of maybe five years once they’re “activated” with a soul. Not that that really matters to Seele, since it’s not like they were planning on using them after 2015 anyways. It’s been a long while since I watched NGE, and while I always assumed that the cores can’t be replaced… is this ever actually mentioned in the show? For all we know, Unit 3 had never been activated before and didn’t have a pilot before Toji.

[]: Re: What if Asuka was a clone in NGE?             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Fri Sep 03, 2021 1:57 pm An Evangelion is a physical god, and therefore, its "service life" should be in the millions if not billions of years. Adam and Lilith lasted for billions of years, after all. It seems like Nerv had the soul of Toji's mother available and entered it into the empty core of Eva-03, no core-swapping necessary. RussianRiz wrote: It would change some central interpretive aspects that we have as a base today. I think with that we could even go so far as to imply that Shinji is a clone too. Maybe we would get a new cyberpunk narrative? Just like Ghost in the Shell?

[]: Re: What if Asuka was a clone in NGE?             Quote Post by Axx°N N. » Fri Sep 03, 2021 2:21 pm UrsusArctos wrote: Maybe we would get a new cyberpunk narrative? Just like Ghost in the Shell?

[]: Re: What if Asuka was a clone in NGE?             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Fri Sep 03, 2021 2:47 pm That was also true of Mamoru Oshii's Ghost in the Shell and that awful Ghost in the Shell 2: Innocence, except that Oshii swapped out the quasi-mystical references to Buddhism with Christian references. It was Kenji Kamiyama who removed all mysticism from Ghost in the Shell, and that makes SAC, 2nd Gig and Solid State Society some of the best adaptations of the franchise I've seen. A cyberpunk Asuka working under Kusanagi is an idea that I've always found fascinating although I can't put my finger on why . I could never properly finish my fanfic take on it!

[]: Re: What if Asuka was a clone in NGE?             Quote Post by Axx°N N. » Fri Sep 03, 2021 3:36 pm Man, Innocence is so polarizing. I either see it called the worst or best ever. As for me, it's my favorite piece of GITS media. As for SAC, I think 2nd Gig is incredible, but 1st Gig and SSS are let-downs in terms of execution matching ambition. I can see why that would be an interesting concept, and maybe it's because they have a lot of similarities--both Asuka and Motoko are characters who crave autonomy but, even as they achieve excellence, find themselves heavily restricted by rigid structures. They experience conflicting dual motivations and values. The idea that Motoko's body doesn't belong to her in both an existential and legal sense overlaps quite well with the experiences of a completely fabricated human working for a shadowy organization--they're more or less the same dilemma.
